era_context: Continuation of the Old Kingdom's final phase in Egypt (Dynasty VI). Naram-Sin's Akkad Empire remains dominant in Mesopotamia. The Harappan civilization continues at its mature peak. This year falls within a period of relative stability in the Old World, though underlying stresses (climate, resource competition, administrative fragmentation) were building.

events.title: Continuation of Pepi II's reign in Egypt
events.description: Pepi II's reign continued during this year. Administrative records from this period show increasing autonomy of provincial governors and the growth of private tomb-building among the elite, indicating a shift in power away from the pharaonic center. The Old Kingdom's characteristic centralization was eroding, though the state apparatus remained functional.

sources.name: Autobiographical inscriptions from provincial officials (e.g., Harkhuf's inscriptions at Aswan)

events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for this particular year. The dating is conventional and carries the same ±50–100 year uncertainty as Pepi II's reign generally.

events.title: Akkad Empire under Naram-Sin: administrative consolidation
events.description: Naram-Sin's administration was consolidating control over conquered territories. Akkadian administrative practices and language were being imposed across Mesopotamia and the Levant. This period saw the development of a more centralized bureaucracy, though regional resistance and the eventual rise of the Gutians would destabilize this system within a generation.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but no specific events are recorded for this particular year. The characterization is based on the broader pattern of his reign.

events.title: Harappan trade networks active
events.description: The Harappan civilization maintained extensive trade networks with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. Seals and artifacts from Indus sites have been found in Mesopotamian contexts, and vice versa. This trade was crucial to the Harappan economy and to the supply of raw materials (lapis lazuli, carnelian, timber) to Mesopotamian elites.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Indus seals and artifacts in Mesopotamian sites (e.g., Ur, Lagash)

events.certainty_note: Trade between the Indus Valley and Mesopotamia is well-established archaeologically. The specific year is not documented, but trade was continuous during the mature Harappan phase.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are recorded for this year in any tradition. The entries above are generalizations about the broader period. This highlights the sparsity of year-by-year documentation for the 3rd millennium BCE.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially undocumented in the historical record. The entries represent inferences from broader patterns in the archaeological and epigraphic record. The absence of specific events for this year is typical for the 3rd millennium BCE; year-by-year precision is rarely possible outside of later periods with more detailed administrative records.
